reviews.review_text:
DEVOUR is one of those co-op horror games that is way more fun than it probably should be. 
The gameplay is simple - collect items, complete the ritual, avoid getting murdered by a demonically possessed cult leader - but with friends it becomes absolute chaos in the best possible way.

The atmosphere is surprisingly tense, especially during later stages when the enemy becomes faster and more aggressive. Communication and teamwork actually matter, and panic starts kicking in once everything begins going wrong.

The good:
- Great co-op horror experience
- Genuinely stressful and chaotic gameplay
- Playing with friends is hilarious
- Simple gameplay loop that works well
- Different maps keep the game fresh
- Good atmosphere and sound design
- Difficulty can get intense in a fun way
- Easy game to jump into for quick sessions

The bad:
- Gameplay becomes repetitive after a while
- Not nearly as fun when playing solo
- AI can feel unfair at times
- Random teammate deaths can snowball the run
- Limited mechanics outside the core gameplay loop
- Some maps are much better than others
- Jump scares lose their impact after a few matches
- Can become frustrating on higher difficulties

Overall, DEVOUR knows exactly what kind of game it wants to be. It’s not the deepest horror game out there, but if you want a chaotic co-op horror experience with friends, it absolutely delivers.

We need more levels
is a good game, scary and difficult 
my friend an i completed this game in nightmare, it was fun, but.... aaaaaaaaaaa more levels please.. 
but yeah, good game if you want to play it with someone <3

The core gameplay simply isn't good. It's scary for the first 20 minutes, then devolves into a stream of repetitive enemies and the level's main monster seeing you from across the map, chasing you, and then screaming in your face. It's the same thing over and over again.

Hugely reliant on jumpscares, which lose their effect pretty fast.

Funner with friends, but what isn't? Be forewarned, this game is pretty balanced around a 4-person group. I don't think it was really intended for 1-3 players.

Also surprises me that a game that has been out so long and received so many updates has so much jank. It feels like a mid-2010s indie horror game.

DEVOUR by Straight Back Games is a fast-paced co-op horror game where 1–4 players must complete occult rituals while avoiding increasingly aggressive possessed cultists. The game excels at creating panic through its intense atmosphere, jump scares, and strong emphasis on teamwork. With multiple maps, escalating difficulty, and a very positive Steam reception (around 91% positive reviews), it offers excellent value for horror fans.

Short verdict: A highly entertaining co-op horror game that delivers genuine tension, screams, and laughter with friends.

Scary group horror game that's actually challenging.

Great game. Very fun to play with friends and it is very re-playable.
Great horror game for horror fans! Super fun co-op with your friends

Repetitive, every level is just burn 10 things, then burn 10 things again, and again, and again... Monster feels unfair and the only counter is a 3 second stun, but the monster outruns you. So you have to rely on cheese spots where it cant get you and just camp there while the other plays do the objective and you can res them. Skip this one imo.

Somehow level 2 is harder than any other level in the game but its still great! I love collecting rats and frying them or washing off random spider eggs in a mystery pool liquid!

Esse jogo é incrível! Com os amigos causa mais discórdia que o Uno pois cada um tem uma função e quando um não consegue cumprir, dificulta tudooo!! Traçar estratégias e uma boa comunicação pode salvar sua gameplay!!! Altamente recomendado pra jogar com os amigos.
i enjoy this game with or without friends! its amazeballs 10/10 game. would recommend my other friends to play and download heh.
A solid horror experience you can play with your friends. The base gameplay is very hard and usually unforgiving, but it makes things really intense as you keep progressing.

This game is an absolute masterpiece. Personally, I have spent around 200 hours playing it at the time of writing this review, and I am more than willing to spend another 200. The game absolutely nailed the balancing of its difficulty modes, and because of that, players of all skill levels can find a place here. As a completionist myself, I can confidently say that the core gameplay loop of DEVOUR feels nearly perfect. The first few runs can definitely be rough, and that comes from personal experience, but once you start understanding the mechanics and maps, you genuinely begin to feel unstoppable. At least until you increase the difficulty again.
That being said, I do not consider DEVOUR perfect when it comes to achievements. Some of them require a huge amount of repetitive grinding, especially achievements like burning hundreds of goats or replaying easier maps over and over again. After a while, that part can become pretty repetitive and tiring.
However, if you are a more casual player who is not planning to 100 percent the game, then this is not really something you need to worry about. And even if you do want to complete everything, my advice would be to take your time and enjoy the gameplay itself instead of focusing only on the completed checklist.

- Whenever you are caught & killed by the monster the audio is EXTREMELY loud, it is disproportionately loud compared to the rest of the game's audio, so if you lower the game volume you won't be able to hear anything just to make the screams tolerable, my ears actually started to hurt. You only have the option to mute the monster's screams instead of having a separately volume slider for it.

The gameplay of Devour felt really intense for me. Not that it was too scary, more like it ramped up the spooky noise and effect levels way too quickly and to the point of overstimulation. I didn't feel like I had the opportunity to appreciate much atmosphere. My friends and I dropped off playing this pretty quick; I'd give it a pass.
